coredata.dc:title: A silica nanoparticle-based dual-responsive ratiometric probe for visualizing hypochlorite and temperature with distinct fluorescence signals
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Jian
Zhang, Chonghua
Lv, Kun
Wang, Hong
Zhang, Peisheng
Yi, Pinggui
Jiang, Jianhui
coredata.dc:description: Hypochlorite (ClO−) and temperature play crucial roles in a wide range of physiological processes, and they are also implicated in various diseases, including cancer, inflammation of tissues and so on. Therefore, it is of great importance to explore a novel method to detect ClO− and temperature instantly. In this study, we developed a silica nanoparticle-based dual-responsive ratiometric fluorescent sensor (DRFS), whose correlative dual emissions can response to ClO− and temperature independently and sensitively. The detection limit of DRFS can reach to as low as 26nM for the detection of ClO−. And further research demonstrates that DRFS possesses excellent anti-interference feature when other possible interferents exist, and has been successfully applied in ClO− detection in human serum and recognition of exogenous/endogenous ClO− in HeLa cells and macrophages by fluorescence microscopic imaging. Moreover, DRFS can also be used as a ratiometric temperature sensor, and the fluorescence intensity ratio (I576/I445) exhibits a linear temperature response in the range from 20 to 60°C with a change ratio as large as a factor of 5. Based on the above research, the DRFS can be used as versatile fluorescence sensor in various physiological and environmental systems.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Silica nanoparticle
Ratiometric fluorescent sensor
Dual-response
Hypochlorite
Temperature

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of fluorescent sensor (DRFS) based on FSNP and RBITC
Synthesis and characterization of the sensor DRFS
Spectral response of DRFS to ClO−
Determination of ClO− in human serum
Ratiometric temperature response of DRFS sensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic presentation of novel DRFS for ClO− and temperature sensing in water.
UV–vis absorption spectra (A) and FL spectra (B) of FSNP, BRITC and DRFS in deionized water.
The AFM image (A) and diameter distribution (B) of as-prepared DRFS.
Fluorescence titration spectra of BRITC (A) and FSNP (B) in deionized water upon gradual addition of ClO−.

Fluorescence (A) and UV–vis absorption (B) titration spectra of DRFS in deionized water upon gradual addition of ClO− from 0 to 200μM; (C) the FL intensity ratio (I445/I576) of DRFS water solution versus ClO− concentration ranging from 0 to 200μM; (D) the linear relationship between FL intensity ratio (I445/I576) and the ClO− concentration from 0 to 2.0μM.
(A) Selectivity of DRFS water solution to ClO− in the presence of potential interference analytes; (B) anti-interference of DRFS water towards various potential interference analytes.
Images of HeLa cells after being incubated with DRFS solution in the absence (A) and presence (B) of ClO− (20uM). The scale bar is 20μm.
Images of RAW 264.7 macrophage cells after being incubated with DRFS solution in the absence (A) and presence (B) of LPS and PMA. The scale bar is 20μm.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of ratiometric temperature-dependent DRFS at different temperature ranging from 20°C to 60°C; (B) the linear relationship between FL intensity ratio (I576/I445) and temperature ranging from 20°C to 60°C.
Determination of ClO− in human serum.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: In the test solutions, human serum was firstly mixed with four-fold ethanol and stirred for 5h. Then, the soluble protein in it was removed by centrifugation (10,000rpm/min), and the supernatant human serum solution was 100-fold diluted before used.

head.ce:title: A silica nanoparticle-based dual-responsive ratiometric probe for visualizing hypochlorite and temperature with distinct fluorescence signals

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Theoretical Organic Chemistry and Functional Molecule of Ministry of Education, Hunan Provincial Key Laboratory of Controllable Preparation and Functional Application of Fine Polymers, Hunan Province College Key Laboratory of QSAR/QSPR, Institute of Functional Materials, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan University of Science and Technology, Xiangtan, Hunan, 411201, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Theoretical Organic Chemistry and Functional Molecule of Ministry of Education
Hunan Provincial Key Laboratory of Controllable Preparation and Functional Application of Fine Polymers
Hunan Province College Key Laboratory of QSAR/QSPR
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Hunan University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics, Hunan University, Changsha 410082, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Key Laboratory of Theoretical Organic Chemistry and Functional Molecule of Ministry of Education, Hunan Provincial Key Laboratory of Controllable Preparation and Functional Application of Fine Polymers, Hunan Province College Key Laboratory of QSAR/QSPR, Institute of Functional Materials, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan University of Science and Technology, Xiangtan, Hunan, 411201, China.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A dual-response ratiometric fluorescent biosensor based on silica nanoparticle was synthesized. The sensor exhibited the attractive property of independent dual-response, is not only a ratiometric fluorescent sensor for sensitively and selectively detecting ClO−, but also a ratiometric fluorescence thermometers with good cycling capability towards temperature. More importantly, the detection limit of sensor for detecting ClO− can reach as low as 26nM, and the sensor has been proved to be a an effective and outstanding fluorescent ClO− sensor in biological system (human serum) and for microscopic cell imaging.

Hypochlorite (ClO−) and temperature play crucial roles in a wide range of physiological processes, and they are also implicated in various diseases, including cancer, inflammation of tissues and so on. Therefore, it is of great importance to explore a novel method to detect ClO− and temperature instantly. In this study, we developed a silica nanoparticle-based dual-responsive ratiometric fluorescent sensor (DRFS), whose correlative dual emissions can response to ClO− and temperature independently and sensitively. The detection limit of DRFS can reach to as low as 26nM for the detection of ClO−. And further research demonstrates that DRFS possesses excellent anti-interference feature when other possible interferents exist, and has been successfully applied in ClO− detection in human serum and recognition of exogenous/endogenous ClO− in HeLa cells and macrophages by fluorescence microscopic imaging. Moreover, DRFS can also be used as a ratiometric temperature sensor, and the fluorescence intensity ratio (I576/I445) exhibits a linear temperature response in the range from 20 to 60°C with a change ratio as large as a factor of 5. Based on the above research, the DRFS can be used as versatile fluorescence sensor in various physiological and environmental systems.

ce:section.ce:para: Hypochlorite (ClO−) is widely existed in the biological and environmental systems [1–3], which plays an important role in our daily life. For example, ClO− is usually selected as a domestic detergent or a disinfectant for dealing with drinking water, treating wastewater, swimming pool water and others [4–7]. ClO− is also recognized as natural defense in living organisms due to its prominent antimicrobial properties [8,9] and involved with a wide variety of biological behaviors. However, excessive generation of ClO− conceals huge health threat to human and animals [10–13]. Experimental investigation has ascertained that superabundant intake ClO− can result in tissue damage and diseases like lung injury [14], atherosclerosis [15], arthritis [16], neuron degeneration [17] and even cancers [18,19]. On the other hand, temperature, which is one of pivotal physical parameters, plays an important role in chemical and biological systems, such as cellular behaviors and chemical equilibrium [20,21]. The normal metabolism of all cells requires a proper temperature to regulate. However, acatastatic temperature is tightly associated with various pathological cellular dysfunctions [22], which may lead to many diseases such as inflammation of tissues, and cancer [23]. Therefore, it is extremely vital to explore novel strategies to effectively and accurately detect ClO− and temperature instantly.

Nowadays, a large number of fluorescent sensors for ClO− or temperature with single emission have been reported [24–31]. Among these traditional sensors [32], they usually suffer from a severe limitation. For example, the change of emission peak intensity is easily influenced by various factors including the efficiency of instruments, sensor concentration, external quenchers and complex environmental conditions [33]. These factors would induce a low signal to noise ratio and less efficiency of detection [34,35]. By contrast, ratiometric dual-emission fluorescent sensors can greatly alleviate the above limitations of one emission signal-based measurements due to they do not suffer from deficiencies like stoichiometric imbalance, which will minimize background signals and optimize the detectability of different signals [36–40].

To date, a variety of ratiometric fluorescent sensors for ClO− or temperature have been already reported [41–48]. Consideration of high correlation of ClO− and temperature in various ranges of physiological processes and diseases, it is most desirable to develop a multifunctional sensor for real-time detection of them. However, a ratiometric fluorescent sensor with dual-response (ClO− and temperature) capability in aqueous solutions is still scarce. Therefore, it is highly commendatory to construct ratiometric dual-emission fluorescent sensors with bifunctional sensing properties like ClO− and temperature.

In this paper, we report on the fabrication of a new silica nanoparticle-based fluorescent sensor, which can be used as a dual-response ratiometric fluorescent sensor (DRFS) for detecting ClO− and temperature independently and effectively in aqueous solution. The sensor DRFS is consisted of fluorescent silica nanoparticle (FSNP, as hypochlorite sensing unit) and rhodamine B isothiocyanate (RBITC, as temperature sensing unit) (Scheme S1). One of the fluorescent moieties is immune from external environment to be a stable reference signal, while the other one can be activated by external stimuli. As illustrated in Scheme 1 , for this sensing platform, the fluorescence quenching phenomenon of the FSNP is observed with addition of strong oxidant ClO−, while emission intensity from RBITC remains nearly unchanged, resulting in the ratiometric detection of ClO− in aqueous solution. In addition, the as-prepared DRFS is also used as an excellent ratiometric fluorescent sensor for accurately monitoring of temperature ranging from 20 to 60°C.

Lipopolysaccharide (LPS) and phorbol myristate acetate (PMA), 3-Aminopropyltrimethoxysilane (APTES), trisodium citrate, rhodamine B isothiocyanate (RBITC), l-cysteine (Cys), glutathione (GSH), Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), tert-butyl hydroperoxide (TBHP), hypochlorous acid (HClO), sodium hypochlorite (NaClO), and ferrous sulfate (FeSO4) are purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Nickelous sulfate (NiSO4·6H2O), calcium chloride anhydrous (CaCl2), sodium chloride (NaCl), cobaltous nitrate (Co(NO3)2·6H2O), potassium chloride (KCl), Magnesium chloride (MgCl2), Manganese sulfate (MnSO4), Cupric sulfate (CuSO4·5H2O), Zinc chloride (ZnCl2) and Mercury(II) chloride (HgCl2) are of analytical grade reagents. The water used in the whole experiments is dealt with double-distillation and then further purified by a Milli-Q water purification system.

In this work, the preparation of FSNP through hydrothermal method was conducted as follows. Firstly, 0.4g trisodium citrate was dispersed in 10mL deionized water in a 25mL two-necked flask, then the transparent solution was stirred for 30min with the continuous aeration of nitrogen gas of high purity to remove oxygen. Secondly, 2.5mL APTES was injected into nitrogen-saturated solution and stirred additional 15min. The mixture solution was transferred into a teflon-lined autoclave of 30mL and sealed with a stainless steel cover. The reaction kettle was placed in an oven at 160°C and incubated for 5h. The reaction solution was cooled to room temperature by natural cooling. Finally, the excessive impurities, such as trisodium citrate and APTES, were removed by dialysis process against water with 500Da dialysis bags, and the prepared fluorescent FSNP solution was stored at 4°C for use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of fluorescent sensor (DRFS) based on FSNP and RBITC
ce:section.ce:para: RBITC (5mg) as fully dissolved in 10mL as-prepared FSNP solution. The mixture solution was placed in the dark and stirred for 48h. Afterwards, the unreacted FSNP and RBITC were removed by a dialysis procedure through a porous cellulose membrane (MWCO 500) against the pure water for 24h. The stable RBITC-functionalized FSNP dispersion, a DRFS for hypochlorite recognition and temperature response, was obtained and kept in the dark for use.

Stock solutions of the sensor DRFS were prepared in deionized water. Test solutions were prepared by using the stock solution and diluting with deionized water. The ClO− solution of various concentrations was prepared with deionized water. The other solutions (100μM) were also prepared in deionized water: Cys, GSH, H2O2, TBHP, HClO, Fe2+, Ni2+, Ca2+, Co2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Hg2+, K+ and Na+. For all measurements, the excitation wavelength was 360nm and for hypochlorite recognition and temperature response.
HeLa cells and macrophages were used to evaluate the cytotoxicity of the sensor DRFS by MTT assay according to ISO 10993-5.

The living HeLa cells and RAW 264.7 macrophage cells were cultured in culture medium including 10% FBS, 50 unit/mL penicillin, and 50μg/mL of streptomycin at 37°C. As to cell imaging studies, cells were seeded in a 80-well plate at a density of 100 cells per well in culture medium and maintained at 37°C in a 5% CO2/95% air incubator for 12h. For evaluating the sensing performance of ClO−, the living HeLa cells are incubated with DRFS (10μg/mL) in culture medium for 15min at 37°C and then washed by PBS twice. Then, the living HeLa cells were treated with NaClO (20uM) for another 15min in culture medium at 37°C. All of the fluorescence images of living HeLa cells were recorded by a confocal laser scanning fluorescence microscope setup containing an Olympus IX81 inverted microscope with an Olympus FV1000 confocal scanning system.

For RAW 264.7 macrophage cells, the as-prepared cells were incubated with DRFS (10μg/mL) in culture medium for 15min at 37°C and washed by PBS twice. Afterwards, the macrophage cells were incubated with LPS (1mg/mL) for 5h at 37°C, and then further co-incubated with PMA (1.0mg/mL) and DRFS (10μg/mL) for 15min at 37°C. All the fluorescence images of cells were obtained by a confocal laser scanning fluorescence microscope setup containing an Olympus IX81 inverted microscope with an Olympus FV1000 confocal scanning system after being washed twice.

An atomic force microscope (AFM, Bruker Dimension Icon) was employed to observe the morphology of FSNP. The nanoparticle diameters were determined by the Malvern Nano-ZS90 instrument. The 1H NMR spectra of DRFS were recorded by Bruker AV-II 500MHz NMR spectrometer. UV–vis spectra were recorded via Shimadzu UV-2501PC spectrophotometer at room temperature. Fluorescence spectra were obtained by a Shimadzu RF-5301PC fluorescence spectrophotometer at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of the sensor DRFS
ce:section.ce:para: The briefly synthetic route of the sensor DRFS is described as follows. Firstly, FSNP is prepared through hydrothermal method, and then FSNP is reacted with rhodamine B isothiocyanate (RBITC) to form DRFS. The DRFS is confirmed by 1H NMR spectra (Fig. S1). And their UV–vis and fluorescence spectra further demonstrate that the FSNP and RBITC have been successfully combined (Fig. 1 ). Fig. 2 A shows the atomic force microscope (AFM) image of DRFS, it can be seen that the diameter of the nanoparticles is ranged from 2 to 4nm, which is consistent with that determined by dynamic light scattering (DLS, Fig. 2B). To optimize the performance of the sensor, the influence of the weight ratio (mFSNP/mRBITC) of FSNP and RBITC on sensitivity of sensor is studied, and the result is presented in Fig. S2 and Table S1, for the sample B (mFSNP/mRBITC =20), which is treated with ClO− from 0 to 200μM, the fluorescence intensity of the sensor is quenched down to 20%, while other two are quenched down to 30% (mFSNP/mRBITC =10, Sample C) and 40% (mFSNP/mRBITC =40, Sample A) respectively, indicating that the most appropriate weight ratio (mFSNP/mRBITC) is 20.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spectral response of DRFS to ClO−
ce:section.ce:para: To better understand the response characteristic of DRFS for the recognition of ClO−, fluorescence and absorption spectra were measured in the study. As shown in Fig. 1A, the maximum absorption peak of FSNP and RBITC appeared at about 350nm and 556nm, and their corresponding fluorescence emission peaked at 445nm and 576nm (Fig. 1B), respectively. Upon the addition of ClO− (0–200μM), a dramatic FL intensity decrease of FSNP at about 445nm can be observed, while that of RBITC remain almost unchanged (Fig. 3 ). The fluorescence quenching mechanism is probably due to the redox reaction between the surface amino groups of FSNP and the strong oxidant ClO−, which is consistent with the some previous reports [49–51].

Based on above results, RBITC indicated the chemical inertness for ClO−, which can be acted as an ideal reference signal for internal calibration in the well-designed ratiometric fluorescence sensor DRFS for effectively detecting ClO−. As depicted in Fig. 4 A, in the absence of ClO−, DRFS solution showed two emission peaks at 445 and 575nm; upon the addition of ClO−, the FL intensity of FSNP continuously and regularly decreased with the increase of ClO− concentration (Fig. 4C), while that of RBITC at 576nm showed slightly drop. Accordingly, the absorbance of FSNP at about 350nm continuously decreased upon increasing the concentration of ClO−, while that of RBITC remains nearly unchanged (Fig. 4B). In addition, a good linear relationship (I445/I576 =2.659−0.708×[ClO−], R2 =0.984) was obtained by plotting the I445/I575 ratio versus the concentrations of ClO− with the concentration of ClO− ranged from 0 to 2.0μM (Fig. 4D). The detection limit was calculated to be 26nM by the following equation: detection limit=3δbi/m, where δbi is the standard deviation of blank measurements, and m is the slope between intensity versus ClO− concentration. The detection limit is significantly lower than the content level of ClO− in drinking water and human cells [1–4]. Time response is a vital performance parameter to evaluate a sensing system, the time response of sensor for the recognition of ClO− was meticulously investigated. As depicted in Fig. S3, the I445/I575 ratio quickly gone down to around 0.7 in 50s and then kept in constant, which showed a rapid time response of the sensor when senses ClO− under experimental conditions. All these results demonstrated the DRFS can be served as a promising candidate for ratiometric fluorescence detection of ClO− in living cell and drinking water.

To evaluate the selectivity and anti-interference of the sensor DRFS towards ClO−, a variety of relevant analytes including biospecies (Cys, Gsh), ROS species (HO, NO, TBHP, t-BuO, 1O2) and various familiar metal ions (Ni2+, Ca2+, Na+, Co2+, K+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Hg2+ and Fe2+) were tested in pure water and the results are displayed Fig. 5 . As shown in Fig. 5A, the I445/I576 ratio kept nearly unchanged in the presence of other analytes. However, the ClO− can significantly promote the quenching of the fluorescence intensity from DRFS sample, even when it coexisted with a mass of other analytes, and lead to dramatic decline of I445/I576 ratio (Fig. 5B). The results reveal that the DRFS is capable of specifically recognizing ClO− in a complex sample matrix.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of ClO− in human serum
ce:section.ce:para: To study the practical application in biological systems, we make an attempt to evaluate whether the sensor DRFS could effectively determine ClO− in human serum samples. In this investigation, different concentrations of ClO− were added to the serum samples, a good recovery was observed. As depicted in Table 1 , the recovery rates are in range of 94.0–96.0%, suggesting that a prominent performance of the sensor DRFS for accurately sensing ClO− in human serum.

The desirable fluorescence properties of DRFS for ClO− prompt us to explore its further biological applications in detection of intracellular ClO−. For cell imaging, a series of MTT assays are implemented to evaluate the cytotoxicity of the DRFS towards HeLa cells and macrophages. As demonstrated in Fig. S4, the cell viability of the HeLa cells and Raw 264.7 macrophages cells exhibits unobvious fluctuation upon addition of DRFS from 0 to 1.0mg/mL. Therefore, it is reasonable that the as-prepared DRFS is an outstanding candidate for cell imaging.

We first investigate whether DRFS could detect exogenous ClO− in living HeLa cells (Fig. 6 ). As shown in Fig. 6A, after the living HeLa cells are incubated with DRFS (10μg/mL) contained culture medium for 15min, strong blue and red fluorescence can be observed inside the cells, and the overlay of blue and red channel fluorescence images shows violet color. Then, the living HeLa cells are treated with NaClO (the donor of ClO−, 20μM) for another 15min, the intensity of blue fluorescence decreases dramatically while the strong red fluorescence hardly changes (Fig. 6B). The above experimental phenomena implies that the sensor DRFS with good cell-permeability holds prominent properties for ratiometric detection of exogenous ClO− in living HeLa cells.

Then, we research on whether the sensor DRFS is capable of detecting intracellular endogenous ClO−. It has been well documented that living RAW 264.7 macrophages cells may generate endogenous ClO− when stimulated by lipopolysaccharide (LPS) and phorbol myristate acetate (PMA) [9,14]. As illustrated in Fig. 7 , in the control group, the living RAW 264.7 macrophage cells are incubated with DRFS (10μg/mL) in culture medium for 15min at 37°C, and exhibit bright blue and red fluorescence (Fig. 7A). By contrast, after the RAW 264.7 macrophage cells have been incubated with LPS (1mg/mL) for 5h, and then further co-incubated with PMA (1.0mg/mL) and DRFS (10μg/mL) for 15min, obvious decrease of blue fluorescence could be detected in the cells while the red fluorescence nearly unchanges (Fig. 7B). And the merged picture confirms that the fluorescence signals are mainly located in the intracellular area. The result reveals that DRFS is capable of imaging endogenous ClO− in the living RAW 264.7 macrophage cells. Thus, there are enough evidences to indicate that the DRFS can be a good sensing platform for intracellular ClO− imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric temperature response of DRFS sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The temperature is a momentous physical parameter that influences functions of chemical and biological systems, for instance reaction rate, molecular structure, cell division, gene expression and enzyme reaction [20–23]. The temperature sensing in chemical and biological systems has attracted increasing interest for the great potential in biological and environmental applications.

To perform the feasibility of DRFS for sensing temperature, the responsibility of FSNP to temperature is firstly studied, as shown in Fig. S5. It is clearly seen that FSNP shows almost no response to temperature fluctuation (20–60°C). Subsequently, the interaction between DRFS and temperature is studied by fluorescence spectroscopy. As revealed in Fig. 8 A, the FL intensity of DRFS at around 576nm decrease gradually and regularly with increasing the temperature, while that of DRFS at 445nm are nearly unchanged. In addition, a good linear relationship is obtained between fluorescence intensity ratio (I576/I445) and temperature (20–60°C) by plotting the logarithmic of fluorescence intensity ratio (I576/I445) versus temperature (Fig. 8B), suggesting that DRFS can be served as an excellent sensor for ratiometric detection of temperature. It has been well documented that the fluorescence intensity of rhodamine B decreased linearly with elevated temperature [52,53]. This property is due to the changes in ratio (photons emitted/photon absorbed), which depends on the rotation of diethylamino groups on the xanthene ring, resulting in the decrease in the quantum yield with increasing temperature [52,53]. The reproducibility property of DRFS is further tested by alternatively varying the temperature between 20 and 60°C (Fig. S6). Such a reversible cycle could be repeated at least for five times by the alternate change of temperature between 20 and 60°C, indicating that DRFS holds preeminent cycling capability, and is an ideal ratiometric fluorescence probe for reversibly monitoring temperature.

In summary, a dual-response ratiometric fluorescent sensor (DRFS) based on silica nanoparticle is synthesized. Compared with previously reported fluorescence sensors, the as-prepared sensor is possessed of several appealing features, including facile preparation, low cost, short response time, good water solubility and low cytotoxicity. Especially, the DRFS with attractive dual-response character can serve as not only a ratiometric fluorescent sensor for sensitively and selectively detecting ClO−, but also a ratiometric fluorescence thermometer with good cycling capability. More importantly, the detection limit of DRFS for detecting ClO− can reach as low as 26nM, and the DRFS has been proved to be a an effective and outstanding fluorescent ClO− sensor in biological system (human serum) and for microscopic cell imaging. Thus, we expect that this project may offer a new thought for developing low-cost and dual-response ratiometric fluorescence biosensor for biological and environmental applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge financial support of the present work by NSFC (Project no. 51373002 and 51603067), Scientific Research Foundation for the Returned Overseas Chinese Scholars, Open Project Program of State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensingand Chemometrics (Project no. 2013008 and 2016019) and Open Project Program of Key Laboratory for High Performance and Functional Polymer Materials of Guangdong Province (South China University of Technology) (Project no. 20160005).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.05.072.

ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

Dual-emitting quantum dot/auantum rod-based nanothermometers with enhanced response and sensitivity in live cells
J. Am. Chem. Soc.
Pyrene derivative emitting red or near-infrared light with monomer/excimer conversion and its application to ratiometric detection of hypochlorite
ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces
Ratiometric detection and imaging of endogenous hypochlorite in live cells and in vivo achieved by using an aggregation induced emission (AIE)-based nanoprobe
Chem. Commun.
A ratiometric fluorescent probe for in situ quantification of basal mitochondrial hypochlorite in cancer cells
Chem. Commun.
Enhancing the luminescence of carbon dots with a reduction pathway
Chem. Commun.
Green synthesis of carbon dots with down- and up-conversion fluorescent properties for sensitive detection of hypochlorite with a dual-readout assay
Analyst
Carbon dots prepared from citric ccid and urea as fluorescent probes for hypochlorite and peroxynitrite
Microchim. Acta
Thermo-optical characterization of fluorescent rhodamine B based temperature-sensitive nanosensors using a CMOS/MEMS micro-hotplate
Sensor Actuators. B: Chem.
A ratiometric fluorescent molecular probe for visualization of mitochondrial temperature in living cell
Chem. Commun.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Jian Chen (male) is currently an associate professor in the School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan University of Science and Technology, Xiangtan, China. His primary research interests include fluorescent nanomaterials, energy transfer in colloides, chemosensors and biosensors. he has published more than 20 papers in Small, Chemical Communications, Macromolecules, Polymer Chemistry, Sensors and Actuators B, Journal of Physical Chemistry B, and so on.

Chonghua Zhang (female) is currently a postdoctor in Hunan University, Changsha, China. Her primary research interests include fluorescent nanomaterials, chemosensors and biosensors. She has published several papers in Analytical Chemistry, Chemical Communications and so on.

Kun Lv (male) received his bachelor’s degree in 2014 and is currently a postgraduate in the School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan University of Science and Technology, researching fluorescent nanomaterials.

Hong Wang (male) received his bachelor’s degree in 2016 and is currently a postgraduate in the School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan University of Science and Technology, researching fluorescent nanomaterials. He has published one paper in Sensors and Actuators B.

Peisheng Zhang (male) is currently a lecturer in the School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan University of Science and Technology, Xiangtan, China. His primary research interests include biosensing and chemometrics. He has published more than 10 papers in Biomaterials, Chemical Communications, Polymer Chemistry, and so on.

Pinggui Yi (male) is currently a professor in the School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan University of Science and Technology, Xiangtan, China. His primary research interests include functional molecules and materials, proton transfer, chemosensors and biosensors. he has published more than 60 papers in Journal of Physical Chemistry B, Chemical Physics Letters, The Journal of Chemical Physics, and so on.

Jianhui Jiang (male) is currently a professor in the School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan University, Changsha, China. His primary research interests include biosensing and chemometrics. he has published more than 100 papers in Journal of the American Chemical Society, Angewandte Chemie-International Edition, Acs Nano, Analytical Chemistry, Chemical communications, Small, and so on.
